title: Ethics in Sociological Research
examTip: Ethics questions often ask you to assess a specific study — always identify the ethical issue, explain why it matters, then evaluate: was it justified? Reference BSA/BPS guidelines and named studies (Milgram, Humphreys) for high marks.
introTips: BSA = British Sociological Association; BPS = British Psychological Society — both publish research ethics guidelines.
Informed consent is the most fundamental principle — all others flow from it.
Deception is sometimes justified, but only if: no other way to get the data, harm is minimal, and debriefing happens afterwards.
intro: Research involving people isn't like testing chemicals in a lab — the subjects of sociological research have feelings, rights, and lives that can be affected by what researchers do. If a sociologist's work damages someone, invades their privacy, or manipulates them without their knowledge, it undermines both the individual and the credibility of sociology as a whole. That's why ethics aren't just good manners — they're fundamental to what research is for.

keyPoints.heading: Why Ethics Matter in Research
keyPoints.content: Sociological research raises ethical questions about consent, privacy, potential harm, honesty, and the power relationship between researcher and participant. Professional ethical guidelines exist to: protect participants from physical and psychological harm, maintain public trust in sociology as a discipline, ensure that research communities (e.g., schools, prisons, ethnic minority groups) remain accessible for future researchers. The British Sociological Association (BSA) and British Psychological Society (BPS) publish detailed ethical guidelines that sociologists are expected to follow. The consequences of unethical research are severe — individuals can be damaged, communities can be stigmatised, and groups may refuse to cooperate with future researchers. Classic examples of unethical research: Stanley Milgram's obedience experiments (1963) — participants were made to believe they had given potentially lethal electric shocks to another person, causing extreme psychological distress; Laud Humphreys' 'Tearoom Trade' (1970) — covert study of gay sex in public toilets in which Humphreys noted car registration plates, traced participants' addresses, and interviewed them in disguise about their home lives — without any consent.
writeBullets: BSA (British Sociological Association) and BPS (British Psychological Society) publish ethics guidelines
Ethics protect participants, maintain trust, and keep communities accessible to future research
Milgram (1963) — extreme distress caused to participants who believed they were giving electric shocks
Humphreys 'Tearoom Trade' (1970) — covert study with no consent; participants traced and interviewed in disguise
Unethical research can permanently damage access to communities for all future sociologists
Power imbalance between researcher and participant must be acknowledged and managed
keyPoints.memTip: Ethics = protecting people AND protecting sociology's reputation to do future research.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Milgram and Humphreys are both examinable as examples of unethical research — ensure students can explain why each was problematic.

keyPoints.heading: Informed Consent
keyPoints.content: Informed consent means participants must be told: what the research is about, who is conducting it and for what purpose, how the data will be used and stored, and their rights as a participant. They must then voluntarily agree to take part — not under duress, pressure, or manipulation. Special considerations apply for vulnerable groups: children require parental or guardian consent (and the child should also assent where possible); people with cognitive impairments may need supported consent processes; prisoners face a power imbalance (they may feel unable to refuse); people in crisis (e.g., domestic violence victims) may be particularly susceptible to pressure. Problems with informed consent: in covert observation, informed consent is by definition impossible — the researcher argues this is justified because (a) the value of the research outweighs the ethical cost, (b) those in public spaces have reduced expectations of privacy. Retrospective consent: sometimes researchers seek consent after covert research has been conducted. Gatekeeper consent: in institutional settings, gatekeepers (e.g., headteachers, prison governors, police chiefs) give permission for research — but this is their consent, not the consent of every individual in the setting.
writeBullets: Informed consent — participants must know the purpose, researcher, and their rights, and voluntarily agree
Children — require parental/guardian consent AND child assent where possible
Prisoners — power imbalance means they may feel unable to refuse; consent may not be truly free
Covert observation — informed consent is impossible by definition
Retrospective consent — seeking consent after covert research has already been done
Gatekeeper consent — institution's permission ≠ individual participant consent
keyPoints.memTip: Informed consent = KNOW + CHOOSE freely. Both conditions must be met — knowing but being pressured doesn't count.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The distinction between gatekeeper consent and participant consent is a nuanced point worth one or two marks in higher-level questions.

keyPoints.heading: Confidentiality, Anonymity and the Right to Withdraw
keyPoints.content: Confidentiality: information shared with the researcher should not be passed to others without the participant's permission. The researcher must store data securely and not share identifiable information with third parties. Anonymity: participants should not be identifiable in published research reports — researchers use pseudonyms (fake names) and change or omit identifying details (specific workplace, town, distinctive personal characteristics). Tension: in very small or specific communities, even anonymised accounts can allow individuals to be identified. Conflict of confidentiality: if a participant reveals illegal activity, risk of harm to self, or risk of harm to others, the researcher faces a dilemma — maintaining confidentiality conflicts with a duty of care (e.g., if a prison researcher hears of a planned assault). Right to withdraw: participants must be told before the study begins that they can stop participating at any time and without penalty — and this right continues throughout the study (ongoing consent). Debriefing: after studies involving any deception, participants must be fully informed about the true nature of the research and given the opportunity to withdraw their data — used after Milgram-type studies.
writeBullets: Confidentiality — information shared with researcher must not be passed to others without permission
Anonymity — participants not identifiable in reports; use pseudonyms and change details
Conflict: if participant reveals illegal activity or harm risk, confidentiality vs duty of care
Right to withdraw — participants can leave at any time without penalty; ongoing consent
Debriefing — after deception, participants must be fully informed and can withdraw their data
Small communities — even anonymised data can identify individuals if the group is small enough
keyPoints.memTip: CARD — Confidentiality, Anonymity, Right to withdraw, Debrief after deception.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The confidentiality vs duty-of-care dilemma is a strong evaluation point in higher-mark questions.

keyPoints.heading: Harm, Deception and Professional Integrity
keyPoints.content: Protection from harm: sociological research should not cause physical or psychological damage to participants. Harm can be direct (a traumatic interview that causes distress) or indirect (a publication that identifies vulnerable people and exposes them to risk). Deception: deliberately misleading participants about the nature, purpose, or conduct of the research — for example, covert observation or false cover stories. Deception is considered ethically acceptable only when: (a) there is no other way to collect the data (the behaviour cannot be studied openly), (b) harm to participants is genuinely minimal, and (c) participants are debriefed fully afterwards. Going native: the risk that a researcher in covert participant observation becomes so absorbed in the group that they lose objectivity — and may even be drawn into illegal or harmful activities. Researcher safety: some research settings (gangs, extremist groups, human trafficking networks) are genuinely dangerous for the researcher. Professional integrity: sociologists must report their findings honestly, even when results challenge their own hypotheses or displease their funders; must not fabricate or selectively present data; must acknowledge the limitations of their methods; must not allow funders (government, corporations) to suppress unflattering findings. James Patrick (1973) left his Glasgow gang study incomplete because he feared for his safety — an extreme example of researcher vulnerability.
writeBullets: Protection from harm — research must not cause physical or psychological damage to participants
Deception — acceptable only if: no other way to collect data, harm is minimal, debrief follows
Going native — researcher loses objectivity and may participate in harmful or illegal activities
Researcher safety — dangerous settings (gangs, extremists) put the researcher at risk
Professional integrity — report findings honestly; do not allow funders to suppress results
James Patrick (1973) — abandoned Glasgow gang study incomplete due to fear for his safety
keyPoints.memTip: The researcher is a human too — ethics protect both the participant AND the sociologist.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Professional integrity is often forgotten but comes up in evaluation of research that produced inconvenient findings.

tables.title: Key Ethical Principles in Sociological Research
rows: Informed consent
Participants know the purpose and voluntarily agree to take part
Respects autonomy; prevents exploitation
Impossible in Humphreys' 'Tearoom Trade' (1970) — covert study
Confidentiality
Information shared with researcher not passed to others
Protects privacy and trust; encourages honesty
Patrick (1973) — identities of gang members protected
Anonymity
Participants not identifiable in published reports; use pseudonyms
Prevents harm from identification; protects sensitive groups
Willis (1977) — 'the lads' given pseudonyms in 'Learning to Labour'
Right to withdraw
Participants can leave at any time without penalty
Ensures ongoing consent; prevents coercion
Must be stated at start; debriefing offered at end
Protection from harm
Research should not cause physical or psychological damage
Prevents trauma, stigma, or danger to participants
Milgram (1963) — extreme distress; participants believed they had harmed others
Deception
Misleading participants about purpose or nature of research
Sometimes necessary — justified only if no alternative and harm is minimal
Covert PO (Patrick 1973); Milgram (1963) — debriefing essential afterwards

0.question: Laud Humphreys' 'Tearoom Trade' (1970) is often cited as an example of unethical research. Which principle did it most seriously violate?
options: Informed consent — participants had no idea they were being studied
Protection from harm — participants suffered physical injuries
Right to withdraw — participants were not allowed to leave
Professional integrity — Humphreys fabricated his findings
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Humphreys covertly observed gay men in public toilets, noted their car registrations, traced their home addresses, and interviewed them in disguise — none of them ever consented to be research participants. Informed consent was completely absent.
0.wrongFeedback: Humphreys' participants were not physically hurt, and he didn't fabricate data. The core problem was that none of them ever knew they were being researched. Which principle covers this?

2.question: A participant in an unstructured interview about family violence reveals that they plan to harm their partner. The researcher faces a conflict between two ethical principles — which two?
options: Confidentiality (keeping what was shared private) vs protection from harm (duty of care to the potential victim)
Anonymity (not identifying the participant) vs right to withdraw (letting the participant leave)
Informed consent (the participant agreed to take part) vs professional integrity (reporting findings honestly)
Pilot study (testing the method) vs debriefing (informing after deception)
2.correctFeedback: Correct. The researcher promised confidentiality — but the information reveals a risk of serious harm to a third party. This is a genuine ethical dilemma with no easy answer, and it's one that practising sociologists actually face.
2.wrongFeedback: Think about what the researcher promised (to keep what was shared private) and what the situation now demands (someone could be hurt). Which two principles are pulling in opposite directions?

commonMistakes: Confusing anonymity with confidentiality — anonymity means not being identifiable in reports; confidentiality means not sharing what was told to you. Both can apply simultaneously.
Thinking informed consent is always achievable — it is impossible in covert observation and very difficult with some vulnerable groups.
Saying deception is always unethical — the BSA guidelines allow it in limited circumstances if harm is minimal and debriefing follows.
Forgetting that gatekeepers give institutional permission, not individual consent — a headteacher's permission does not mean all pupils have consented.
Not mentioning professional integrity — researchers must report findings honestly even when funders or authorities dislike the results.
examTips: In ethics questions, always go beyond listing principles — apply them to a specific study or scenario to show understanding rather than just recall.
When evaluating covert observation, use the word 'justified' — examiners want you to weigh the ethical problems against the research value, not just list the problems.
Milgram (1963) and Humphreys (1970) are the two most cited examples of unethical research — know them both and be able to explain which principles they violated.
revisionCardBullets: BSA and BPS — professional bodies that publish ethical guidelines for sociological and psychological research
Informed consent — participants know the purpose and voluntarily agree; impossible in covert observation
Vulnerable groups — children, prisoners, people with cognitive impairments need special consideration
Gatekeeper consent — institution's permission ≠ individual participant consent
Confidentiality — what participants share must not be passed to others without permission
Anonymity — participants must not be identifiable in reports; use pseudonyms
Right to withdraw — participants can leave at any time; debriefing must follow deception
Protection from harm — research must not cause physical or psychological damage
Deception — acceptable only if: no alternative, minimal harm, and debriefing follows
Going native — researcher loses objectivity by becoming too involved in the group
Professional integrity — report findings honestly; do not suppress inconvenient results
Milgram (1963) and Humphreys (1970) — classic examples of unethical research
